skill.name: Cross-Site Scripting and HTML Injection Testing
skill.description: Comprehensive XSS and HTML injection vulnerability assessment skill for authorized security testing. Provides detection techniques, exploitation methods, and remediation guidance for web application security professionals.
skill.summary: Execute client-side injection vulnerability assessments to identify XSS flaws, demonstrate exploitation techniques, and validate input sanitization mechanisms.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill is documentation for authorized XSS and HTML injection security testing. Static analyzer flagged markdown code examples as threats, but these are educational payloads for penetration testers, not executable malicious code. The skill includes proper legal/ethical guardrails requiring written authorization. Risk is low as content is instructional documentation, not functional exploit code.

risk_factor_evidence.reasoning: These lines contain documentation examples of dangerous JavaScript sinks (document.write, eval, innerHTML) that developers should avoid. This is educational content explaining XSS attack vectors, not actual vulnerable code being executed.

These are example XSS payloads demonstrating cookie theft techniques for security testing purposes. The fetch and Image requests shown are payloads that testers inject into vulnerable applications to prove exploitability, not code the skill itself executes.

low_findings.title: Offensive Security Content
low_findings.description: Skill contains detailed instructions for cookie theft, session hijacking, and keylogger injection. While educational, this content could be misused by bad actors without proper authorization frameworks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly documents keylogger and credential stealing payloads. However, it includes proper legal/ethical requirements (lines 29-33, 407-410) mandating written authorization, which mitigates risk.

content.user_title: Test Web Applications for XSS Vulnerabilities
content.value_statement: Web applications face constant XSS attack threats that compromise user sessions and data. This skill provides security professionals with systematic testing methodologies to identify and remediate client-side injection flaws before attackers exploit them.
actual_capabilities: Identify stored, reflected, and DOM-based XSS vulnerabilities in web applications
Test input validation and output encoding mechanisms across different contexts
Demonstrate session hijacking and cookie theft in controlled environments
Validate Content Security Policy effectiveness against injection attacks
Generate proof-of-concept payloads for vulnerability verification
Provide remediation recommendations aligned with OWASP guidelines
limitations: Requires written authorization and defined testing scope before use
Cannot bypass HttpOnly cookie flags or server-side security controls
Limited to client-side vulnerability testing only
Effectiveness depends on target application having user input fields

use_cases.title: Security Audit for Web Applications
use_cases.description: Perform comprehensive XSS vulnerability assessment on client websites before deployment to identify injection flaws that could compromise user data and sessions.
use_cases.target_user: Web security consultants conducting authorized penetration tests

use_cases.title: Developer Security Training
use_cases.description: Use XSS examples and payloads in controlled lab environments to teach development teams about injection vulnerability root causes and prevention techniques.
use_cases.target_user: Security engineers building developer awareness programs

use_cases.title: Bug Bounty Research
use_cases.description: Systematically test in-scope applications for XSS vulnerabilities using documented payloads and bypass techniques to discover reportable security issues.
use_cases.target_user: Bug bounty hunters with authorized scope permissions

prompt_templates.title: Basic XSS Detection
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this web application input field for XSS vulnerabilities. Test with basic payloads like and . Report whether input is reflected and if scripts execute.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: Testing a search box for reflected XSS

prompt_templates.title: Stored XSS Assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Test the comment system on this blog for stored XSS. Create a test account, submit payloads in comment bodies, then verify if they persist and execute for other users. Document the injection point and payload that succeeded.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: Full stored XSS testing workflow

prompt_templates.title: DOM-Based XSS Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Examine the JavaScript code handling URL hash fragments and postMessage events. Identify dangerous sinks like innerHTML and eval that process user-controlled data. Provide exploitation payloads for each vulnerable pattern found.

prompt_templates.title: CSP Bypass Testing
prompt_templates.prompt: The target site has CSP header with script-src 'self' https://cdn.trusted.com. Find JSONP endpoints on the trusted CDN that could be abused to execute arbitrary JavaScript. Test each endpoint with alert(1) callback.

output_examples.input: Test search parameter ?q= for reflected XSS
output_examples.output: VULNERABLE: Input reflected without encoding. Payload executes successfully. The server returns search term in HTML body without HTML entity encoding, allowing arbitrary script execution.

output_examples.input: Analyze comment field for stored XSS with CSP protection
output_examples.output: PROTECTED: While input is stored and reflected, CSP header 'script-src 'self'' blocks inline script execution. Alternative vectors tested (JSONP endpoints, data: URIs) also blocked by CSP. Recommend maintaining current CSP configuration.

output_examples.input: Test DOM XSS in single-page application routing
output_examples.output: VULNERABLE: Route parameter written to innerHTML at line 47 without sanitization. URL /profile/ triggers script execution. Client-side only attack - payload never reaches server logs.

best_practices: Always obtain written authorization defining scope, targets, and data handling procedures before testing
Use isolated test accounts and environments to avoid impacting real users or production data
Document all findings with reproduction steps, severity ratings, and remediation guidance for developers
anti_patterns: Never test XSS payloads on production systems without explicit written authorization from the owner
Do not use worm-like payloads that automatically spread to other users beyond your test scope
Avoid exfiltrating real user data - capture only your own test cookies for demonstration purposes

faq.question: Is this skill legal to use?
faq.answer: Only when you have explicit written authorization from the system owner. Unauthorized XSS testing violates computer crime laws in most jurisdictions. Always define scope, targets, and data handling in a signed agreement before testing.

faq.question: What is the difference between XSS and HTML injection?
faq.answer: XSS involves JavaScript execution in the victim's browser, enabling session theft and actions on behalf of the user. HTML injection only renders HTML content without script execution, typically used for phishing or defacement but with lower impact.

faq.question: Why are payloads failing on modern websites?
faq.answer: Modern frameworks like React, Angular, and Vue automatically escape outputs by default. Additionally, Content Security Policy headers, input sanitization libraries, and WAFs block many traditional XSS vectors. Test multiple bypass techniques and encoding variations.

faq.question: Can this skill test for blind XSS?
faq.answer: Blind XSS requires external callback servers to detect delayed execution when payloads are stored and viewed later by administrators. This skill documents the techniques but you must set up your own callback infrastructure for blind XSS testing.

faq.question: What should I do if I find critical XSS vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: Document the vulnerability with reproduction steps and impact demonstration using alert(1) only - do not steal real data. Report immediately to the system owner per your authorization agreement. Follow responsible disclosure practices.

faq.question: Does this work on mobile apps?
faq.answer: Mobile apps using WebView components can be vulnerable to XSS if they load web content or accept URLs. The same testing principles apply, but you need to intercept app traffic using tools like Burp Suite or Frida for WebView inspection.
